status.state_description: Waiting for human approval of the plan
status.product_description: IoT wearable device for elderly fall detection with real-time caregiver alerts, GPS tracking, and automatic emergency dispatch. Must comply with FDA Class II and IEC 62304 for medical device software.

plan.description: Define product requirements document (PRD) for elder_fall_detection wearable: use cases, user personas (elderly wearer, caregiver, emergency responder), functional requirements (fall detection, GPS, alert dispatch), non-functional requirements (battery life, water resistance, latency SLAs), and success metrics.
acceptance_criteria: PRD covers all three user personas with at least 5 user stories each
Functional requirements include fall detection sensitivity/specificity targets (≥95% sensitivity, ≤2 false positives/day)
Non-functional requirements specify battery life (≥72h), IP67 rating, alert latency (<30s)
Success metrics are measurable and time-bound
Document reviewed and signed off by product_manager and regulatory_specialist

plan.description: Perform preliminary hazard analysis (PHA) and initial risk assessment per ISO 14971. Identify all foreseeable hazards: missed fall detection, false alarms causing unnecessary emergency dispatch, GPS failure, battery depletion, communication failure, data breach exposing patient location. Establish risk acceptability criteria and risk control strategy.
acceptance_criteria: All hazard categories documented with severity, probability, and risk level (S×P matrix)
Risk acceptability criteria defined (ALARP principle applied)
Risk management plan references ISO 14971:2019 and maps to FDA 21 CFR Part 820
At least one risk control measure identified per unacceptable risk
Document traceable to PRD requirements from step 1
ISO 14971 risk management file complete
Residual risk acceptable

plan.description: Establish Design History File (DHF) skeleton and Quality Management System (QMS) artifacts per FDA 21 CFR Part 820 and ISO 13485. Create DHF folder structure, design control plan, document control procedure, and traceability matrix template linking requirements → design → verification → validation.
acceptance_criteria: DHF folder structure matches FDA 21 CFR Part 820.30 sections
Software Development Plan references IEC 62304 software safety class (Class B minimum)
Traceability matrix template has columns: requirement_id, design_element, verification_method, validation_evidence
Document control procedure defines revision control, review, and approval workflow
ISO 13485 QMS procedures list covers: design control, purchasing, production, CAPA, complaint handling
DHF (Design History File) structure created
V&V protocol drafted

plan.description: Produce threat model (STRIDE) and cybersecurity risk assessment per IEC 62443 for the elder_fall_detection system. Identify attack surfaces: BLE wearable-to-phone link, MQTT/HTTPS cloud channel, GPS data stream, caregiver mobile app, backend API. Define security controls and generate initial SBOM.
acceptance_criteria: STRIDE analysis covers all 6 attack surfaces with threat entries
Each threat rated by DREAD or CVSS score
Security controls mapped to each threat (countermeasure coverage ≥90%)
SBOM template follows SPDX 2.3 or CycloneDX 1.4 format
Document references IEC 62443-3-3 security levels
Penetration test plan outlined with scope and methodology
Device identity provisioned
Firmware signing enabled

plan.description: Design caregiver mobile app UX and wearable device interaction model. Deliverables: user journey maps, wireframes for iOS/Android caregiver app (alert dashboard, GPS map view, device status, emergency contact management, alert history), wearable button/LED/haptic interaction design, and accessibility audit for elderly users.
acceptance_criteria: User journey maps cover: fall detected → caregiver alerted → responder dispatched flows
Wireframes produced for all 7 mobile screens in Figma or equivalent
Wearable interaction spec defines LED patterns (≥4 states) and haptic feedback sequences
Accessibility audit passes WCAG 2.1 AA for mobile app
SOS button interaction spec includes accidental press prevention (hold 3s)

plan.description: Design PCB schematic and layout for the wearable device. Components: ARM Cortex-M4 MCU, 6-axis IMU (accelerometer + gyroscope) for fall detection, GPS/GNSS module, BLE 5.0 module, LiPo battery management IC, USB-C charging, RGB LED, haptic motor driver, IP67-rated connector. Generate BOM and run DRC.
acceptance_criteria: Schematic passes ERC (0 errors)
PCB layout passes DRC (0 errors, trace clearances ≥0.15mm)
BOM includes manufacturer part numbers, quantities, and alternatives for all components
IMU placement within 5mm of device center of mass for accurate fall detection
GPS antenna clearance zone respected (>5mm keepout)
Battery protection circuit (overvoltage, overcurrent, short circuit) present

plan.description: Design wearable enclosure CAD model. Requirements: IP67 sealing, comfortable wristband form factor, SOS button placement, USB-C charging port with waterproof cover, LED window, haptic motor mounting, and medical-grade silicone band. Generate STEP/STL files and tolerance analysis.
acceptance_criteria: STEP files pass import into reference CAD tool without errors
Tolerance analysis confirms IP67 O-ring compression within manufacturer spec
SOS button travel distance 1.5mm ± 0.1mm
Assembly instructions have exploded view diagrams and torque specs
Biocompatibility note references ISO 10993 for skin-contact materials
Weight target ≤45g including band

plan.description: Set up the elder_fall_detection solution configuration: SAGE project.yaml, prompts.yaml, tasks.yaml. Configure firmware development toolchain (STM32CubeIDE / CMake + ARM GCC), embedded CI (GitHub Actions with QEMU emulation), MQTT broker (AWS IoT Core), and development environment docker-compose for backend services.

payload.cloud: AWS IoT Core (MQTT) + AWS Lambda + RDS PostgreSQL

acceptance_criteria: project.yaml declares solution_name, active_modules, theme, and compliance_standards
prompts.yaml defines roles: firmware_engineer, safety_engineer, caregiver_app_developer
tasks.yaml includes task types: fall_analysis, gps_update, alert_dispatch, device_health_check
CMake builds firmware for STM32L4 target without errors (hello_world blinky test)
GitHub Actions workflow triggers on firmware/ changes and runs unit tests
docker-compose up starts backend, MQTT broker, and database in <60s

plan.description: Implement FreeRTOS-based firmware for STM32L4 wearable. Tasks: IMU data acquisition task (200Hz sampling, DMA transfer), fall detection algorithm task (threshold + ML-lite inference), GPS NMEA parsing task, BLE GATT server task (custom fall alert service, battery level, device info), power management (sleep/wake), SOS button ISR, LED/haptic control.

tasks.name: imu_task
tasks.description: Read LSM6DSO via SPI DMA at 200Hz

tasks.name: gps_task
tasks.description: Parse u-blox NMEA via UART DMA

tasks.name: ble_task
tasks.description: nRF52840 co-processor UART bridge, GATT notify

acceptance_criteria: All FreeRTOS tasks start without stack overflow on target hardware
IMU samples received at 200Hz ± 2Hz measured via logic analyzer
Fall detection algorithm detects 5/5 scripted fall scenarios in bench test
GPS fix acquired within 60s cold start outdoors
BLE GATT server advertises and pairs with reference phone in <10s
SOS button requires 3s hold, triggers ISR, false trigger <1% with 100 random presses
Device enters low-power sleep when idle, current draw <5mA in sleep mode
All firmware files comply with IEC 62304 software unit identification requirements
IEC 62304 software class documented
SOUP components listed
OTA update mechanism implemented
Watchdog timer configured

plan.description: Develop and validate fall detection ML model. Pipeline: collect/curate labeled IMU dataset (falls vs. ADLs), train lightweight CNN or threshold+feature classifier (suitable for MCU deployment), quantize to INT8, export to TFLite or CMSIS-NN format, validate on held-out test set against sensitivity/specificity targets.

payload.model_type: CNN_1D or LSTM on 2s sliding window (400 samples at 200Hz)
payload.deployment_target: STM32L4 with CMSIS-NN or TFLite Micro

acceptance_criteria: Training dataset ≥5000 labeled fall events and ≥20000 non-fall ADL windows
Model validation on held-out test set: sensitivity ≥0.95, specificity ≥0.90
INT8 quantized model size ≤100KB (fits STM32L4 flash)
Inference latency ≤20ms on STM32L476 at 80MHz
Model card documents training data, evaluation methodology, known limitations
Validation report signed as IEC 62304 software verification evidence

plan.description: Design and implement PostgreSQL schema for elder_fall_detection backend. Tables: devices (device_id, owner, firmware_version, status), users (user_id, role: wearer/caregiver/admin, emergency_contacts), fall_events (event_id, device_id, timestamp, gps_coords, severity, alert_status), gps_history (device_id, timestamp, lat, lon, accuracy), audit_log (actor, action, timestamp, details). HIPAA-compliant schema with encryption at rest and row-level security.

payload.encryption: pgcrypto for PII columns, TDE at RDS level

acceptance_criteria: ERD covers all 5 tables with correct cardinality (devices 1:N fall_events, users M:N devices)
All PII columns (name, phone, GPS coords) encrypted using pgcrypto
Row-level security policy: caregivers can only query devices they are assigned to
Alembic migration runs cleanly on fresh PostgreSQL 15 instance
Indexes on: fall_events(device_id, timestamp), gps_history(device_id, timestamp)
Schema passes SQL linter with 0 warnings

plan.description: Implement FastAPI backend for elder_fall_detection. Endpoints: device registration and provisioning, MQTT ingestion handler (fall events, GPS updates, heartbeat), real-time caregiver alert dispatch (push notification + SMS via Twilio + email), automatic emergency services dispatch (E911/NG911 API integration), GPS history retrieval, alert management (acknowledge, dismiss, escalate), device management, caregiver/user management. Implement agentic coordinator pattern: coordinator agent routes incoming device events to specialist agents (fall analyzer, GPS tracker, alert dispatcher).
acceptance_criteria: POST /devices/register provisions new device and returns certificates for AWS IoT Core
MQTT message ingestion processes fall event and triggers alert within 10s end-to-end
Alert dispatch sends push + SMS within 15s of fall event receipt
Emergency dispatch integrates with RapidSOS API and logs dispatch_id
GET /devices/{id}/location returns latest GPS with timestamp and accuracy
Coordinator agent routes events with ReAct Thought→Action→Observation traces logged
All endpoints return 401 for unauthenticated requests
Backend handles 100 concurrent device connections without timeout

plan.description: Design and document OpenAPI 3.1 specification for the elder_fall_detection backend API. Cover all endpoints from step 12, define request/response schemas, authentication (JWT Bearer + API key for devices), error codes, webhook schema for MQTT device events, and rate limiting policy. Generate client SDK stubs.
acceptance_criteria: OpenAPI spec validates with openapi-spec-validator 0 errors
All 20+ endpoints documented with request/response examples
Authentication schemes documented with example JWT claims
Webhook schema defined for MQTT fall_event, gps_update, heartbeat, sos_press message types
Python client SDK generated from spec and passes import test
Rate limiting headers (X-RateLimit-*) documented on all endpoints

plan.description: Implement caregiver mobile app (React Native or Flutter) for iOS and Android. Screens per UX wireframes (step 5): alert dashboard with real-time push, live GPS map (Google Maps SDK), device status panel (battery, connectivity, firmware version), emergency contact management, alert history with fall event details, device pairing via BLE, notification settings. Implement HIPAA-compliant data display (auto-lock, no screenshots).
acceptance_criteria: All 7 screens render without crashes on iOS 16+ and Android 12+
Push notification received and alert displayed within 20s of fall event
GPS map shows device location with <5s refresh interval
BLE pairing completes device setup in <3 steps
App auto-locks after 5min inactivity (HIPAA control)
Screenshot prevention active on sensitive screens (device status, GPS)

plan.description: Set up production cloud infrastructure for elder_fall_detection on AWS. Components: ECS Fargate for FastAPI backend, AWS IoT Core (MQTT broker, device registry, rules engine), RDS PostgreSQL Multi-AZ, ElastiCache Redis (real-time alert state), CloudFront + S3 (mobile app OTA updates), VPC with private subnets, WAF, CloudWatch monitoring, SNS for alert fanout. IaC with Terraform.
acceptance_criteria: terraform plan produces 0 errors on clean AWS account
terraform apply provisions all resources in <15min
ECS service passes health check and serves /health endpoint
IoT Core MQTT broker accepts device connections with mutual TLS
RDS Multi-AZ failover completes in <60s (tested via RDS reboot with failover)
WAF rules block SQLi and XSS (OWASP managed rule group enabled)
CloudWatch alarm triggers SNS notification on backend error rate >1%
All secrets stored in Secrets Manager (no plaintext credentials in Terraform state)

plan.description: Implement CI/CD pipelines for firmware, backend, and mobile app. Firmware pipeline: build → unit test → static analysis (PC-lint/cppcheck) → MISRA-C check → artifact upload. Backend pipeline: lint → unit test → integration test → Docker build → deploy to ECS staging. Mobile pipeline: lint → test → build IPA/APK → TestFlight/Firebase distribution. Monitoring: CloudWatch dashboards for alert latency, GPS accuracy, device connectivity, fall event throughput.
acceptance_criteria: All 3 CI pipelines pass on main branch with green status
Firmware MISRA-C check produces report with 0 mandatory violations
Backend pipeline achieves ≥80% code coverage

test_categories.description: Algorithm correctness with labeled IMU data

acceptance_criteria: All 95 unit test cases pass with 0 failures

Integration tests use testcontainers (PostgreSQL, Redis, MQTT broker).

Backend achieves ≥85% line coverage

plan.description: Execute end-to-end system test suite covering full elder_fall_detection workflow: device boot → BLE pairing → cloud registration → fall event detection → MQTT publish → backend processing → caregiver alert (push+SMS) → optional emergency dispatch. Test scenarios: happy path, GPS unavailable (indoor), communication loss and recovery, low battery alert, SOS manual trigger, concurrent alerts from 10 devices.

acceptance_criteria: Happy path: fall-to-caregiver-alert latency <30s in 95% of runs (50 repetitions)
GPS unavailable scenario uses last known location with timestamp warning in alert
MQTT reconnect scenario: device reconnects and sends buffered events within 60s
10 concurrent devices: all alerts delivered within 45s, 0 dropped
SOS trigger: emergency dispatch initiated within 15s of button hold
System test report generated as PDF with pass/fail for all scenarios
System test report accepted as IEC 62304 system integration testing evidence

plan.description: Complete DHF with all design verification and validation evidence. Prepare 510(k) submission readiness checklist.

Sections: device description, substantial equivalence comparison to predicate device, performance testing summary, software documentation summary (IEC 62304 compliance), cybersecurity documentation (IEC 62443, FDA cybersecurity guidance), labeling review, and biocompatibility summary (ISO 10993).

acceptance_criteria: 510(k) package includes all FDA required sections per 21 CFR Part 807

plan.description: Complete final FMEA and fault tree analysis (FTA) for elder_fall_detection system incorporating hardware, firmware, and software failure modes. Update risk management report with post-mitigation risk levels. Verify all risk controls implemented and effective via test evidence. Conduct formal hazard analysis update per ISO 14971 post-design-freeze.

Each failure mode has: effect, severity (S), occurrence (O), detection (D), RPN, and mitigation
Fault tree for 'missed fall detection' identifies all contributing root causes
Post-mitigation RPN for all high-risk items reduced by ≥50%
All risk controls verified via specific test evidence references (test ID + result)
Risk management report concludes overall residual risk is acceptable
Document signed by safety_engineer, quality_engineer, and regulatory_specialist
ISO 14971 risk management file complete
Residual risk acceptable

plan.description: Execute security review and finalize SBOM for elder_fall_detection. Run automated vulnerability scan on firmware dependencies (Dependency-Track), backend dependencies (Trivy + pip-audit), and mobile app (MobSF static analysis). Update threat model with findings, document residual risks, and produce final SBOM in CycloneDX 1.4 format for FDA cybersecurity submission.
acceptance_criteria: SBOM covers 100% of firmware, backend, and mobile app dependencies
No known Critical (CVSS ≥9.0) CVEs unmitigated in final SBOM
High CVEs (CVSS 7.0-8.9): all addressed or formally accepted with justification
MobSF scan reports no hardcoded credentials or insecure data storage
Semgrep scan reports 0 injection vulnerabilities in backend API
Penetration test plan scoped for external pentest engagement
Final SBOM in FDA-accepted CycloneDX 1.4 format
Device identity provisioned
Firmware signing enabled

plan.description: Produce final documentation suite for elder_fall_detection: developer README (firmware build, backend setup, mobile setup), architecture decision records (ADRs), API reference (from OpenAPI spec), caregiver user guide, device setup guide (pairing, charging, wearing), system architecture diagram, and data flow diagram showing device → cloud → caregiver alert path.
acceptance_criteria: README has: project overview, quick start (firmware build in <5 steps, backend up in 1 docker-compose command)
Architecture doc covers all 5 system layers: device, BLE, cloud, backend, mobile
At least 5 ADRs covering key decisions: RTOS choice, ML model format, alert channel priority, cloud provider, database choice
Caregiver user guide has screenshots for all 7 app screens
Data flow diagram shows MQTT message path from IMU sample to caregiver push notification
All docs reviewed by technical_writer for clarity and completeness

flaws: ML model (step 10) incorrectly depends on firmware (step 9). Model training requires labeled IMU datasets, not working firmware. This serializes 6+ weeks of parallel work unnecessarily and is a fundamental scheduling error.

BLE-only connectivity architecture is a critical safety gap. If the caregiver's phone is out of BLE range (~10m), the device cannot alert anyone. For a medical safety device, this is a design defect — cellular (LTE-M/NB-IoT) fallback is not optional.

PCB form factor constraint (35mm x 25mm) is likely infeasible. u-blox MAX-M10S alone is 9.6mm x 9.6mm. Fitting STM32L476 + nRF52840 + u-blox MAX-M10S + LSM6DSO + BQ25180 + passives on a 35x25mm PCB while meeting RF keepout zones is implausible without a 6-layer board and 0201 passives. No feasibility analysis justifies this constraint.

Dual-chip architecture (STM32L4 + nRF52840 as co-processor) adds unnecessary BOM cost, PCB area, and inter-chip UART latency. The nRF52840 has its own Cortex-M4 that can run FreeRTOS and handle fall detection. This architectural choice is never justified.

72h battery life with active GPS is physically implausible without duty cycling. u-blox MAX-M10S draws ~10-15mA at acquisition, ~1mA tracking. At 5mA average GPS draw alone on a typical 180mAh wristband battery, GPS exhausts battery in 36h before accounting for MCU, BLE, or cellular. No power budget analysis exists anywhere in the plan.

ML model validation uses MobiAct and SisFall datasets — both collected from younger, healthy adults performing simulated falls in controlled lab conditions. Validation on these datasets does not transfer to real elderly users with atypical gait, slower falls, partial falls, or falls from seated positions. This invalidates the ≥95% sensitivity claim for the target population.

IEC 62366-1 usability engineering is completely absent. FDA requires a Usability Engineering File (UEF) for 510(k) submissions. Missing use error risk analysis, formative studies, and summative validation with elderly users is a submission blocker — not a gap that can be patched post-submission.

No post-market surveillance (PMS) plan. FDA 21 CFR Part 803 (MDR) and 21 CFR Part 806 requires PMS from day one of commercialization. A Class II fall detection device with missed-fall risk will face MDR reporting obligations. This is entirely absent.

RapidSOS/NG911 integration assumes a contractual API relationship that takes months to establish, has geographic coverage gaps in rural US, and requires specific data formatting per NENA i3 standards. Treating it as a simple API call in step 12 acceptance criteria is unrealistic.

Software Safety Class assignment says 'Class B minimum' without justification. For a device where software failure (missed fall) could lead to serious patient harm (death from undetected fall), IEC 62304 Class C is the defensible classification. Class B underclassification risks FDA refusal-to-accept.

MQTT message buffering during connectivity loss is tested in step 19 but never designed. Step 9 firmware spec says nothing about flash-backed event queue, queue depth, TTL, or replay ordering. The system test will fail because the feature was never specified.

No manufacturing process validation. A Class II medical device requires IQ/OQ/PQ for manufacturing processes (pick-and-place, reflow, IP67 seal testing, functional test fixture). This is entirely absent from the plan — the DHF cannot be complete without it.

JWT authentication for IoT devices (step 4, step 12) conflicts with mutual TLS mentioned in the same plan. AWS IoT Core uses X.509 certificates, not JWTs, for device authentication. The authentication architecture is internally inconsistent.

No device provisioning security model at manufacturing time. How are X.509 certificates injected at the factory? No HSM, no certificate authority, no secure element (ATECC608 or equivalent). Firmware signing is listed as an acceptance criterion but the key management infrastructure is never designed.
suggestions: Move step 10 (ML model) to run in parallel with steps 6-9, with only the deployment-to-firmware step blocked on step 9. This recovers 3-4 weeks of critical path time.

Add LTE-M or NB-IoT as the primary alert channel, with BLE as the configuration/pairing interface only. Nordic nRF9161 or Sequans Monarch handles LTE-M and integrates more cleanly than the dual-chip STM32+nRF52840 design.

Commission a 6-layer PCB stack-up analysis before committing to the 35x25mm constraint. If the constraint is from product design requirements, add a feasibility step before PCB layout. If it is aspirational, state it as a target and validate in step 7 (mechanical).

Add an explicit power budget analysis step before PCB design (step 6). Define GPS duty cycle (e.g., 1 fix per 30s when stationary, continuous when motion detected), CPU active/sleep ratios, and BLE advertising intervals. This gates the 72h claim.

Add step 0.5: IEC 62366-1 Usability Engineering Plan, with formative usability studies at step 5 (UX Design) using actual elderly participants, and summative validation scheduled before step 20 (DHF completion).

Add a clinical performance validation step between step 17 (embedded test) and step 20 (DHF). The 510(k) substantial equivalence argument for a fall detection device will be strengthened significantly by even a 30-subject pilot study with elderly users in a care home setting.

Add step 8.5: device identity and certificate provisioning design. Specify secure element (ATECC608B or equivalent), certificate authority architecture, factory provisioning fixture, and certificate revocation mechanism.

Replace IEC 62443 as the primary cybersecurity standard with FDA's 2023 Cybersecurity Guidance (Refuse to Accept policy) and NIST IR 8259. IEC 62443 is industrial control systems — reference it as supplementary only.

Define HIPAA Business Associate Agreements (BAAs) with AWS, Twilio, SendGrid, and RapidSOS explicitly. AWS BAA is available but must be executed. Twilio and SendGrid have their own BAA processes. Missing BAA voids HIPAA safe harbor.

Specify a post-market surveillance plan as a deliverable in step 21 (regulatory). Include MDR decision tree, complaint handling procedure (21 CFR Part 820.198), and field corrective action trigger criteria.
missing: IEC 62366-1 Usability Engineering File — formative studies, use error analysis, summative validation with elderly target users. FDA will not accept a 510(k) without this.
Post-market surveillance plan (21 CFR Part 803, 21 CFR Part 806, EU MDR Article 83 if international scope intended).
Cellular (LTE-M/NB-IoT) connectivity design. BLE range limitation makes the device unsafe as a sole-communication-path medical device.
Power budget analysis and GPS duty cycling strategy supporting the 72h battery life claim.
Manufacturing process validation (IQ/OQ/PQ) for PCB assembly, IP67 seal testing, and final functional test.
Secure element and certificate provisioning architecture for device identity at manufacturing time.
Clinical performance data with elderly subjects — lab datasets alone are insufficient for the 510(k) substantial equivalence performance testing section.
HIPAA BAA execution plan for all third-party data processors (AWS, Twilio, SendGrid, RapidSOS).
Data retention and right-to-deletion policy (HIPAA minimum 6-year retention, GDPR if EU market).
PCB feasibility analysis before committing to 35x25mm constraint.
Alert deduplication logic — what prevents duplicate NG911 dispatch if caregiver and auto-dispatch both trigger within seconds?
Firmware OTA update design (mentioned in step 9 acceptance criteria but never designed as a task — rollback, signature verification, atomic swap, and fail-safe boot are all unspecified).
International regulatory pathway — CE marking under EU MDR 2017/745 is not mentioned despite the device's clear EU market applicability.
security_risks: BLE bonding without a secure element means pairing keys are stored in MCU flash. A physical device compromise yields all pairing credentials and enables location tracking of the patient. No mention of anti-tamper or key erasure on tamper detection.
JWT tokens for caregiver mobile app without refresh token rotation policy. A stolen JWT provides persistent patient location access. Token lifetime and rotation strategy are unspecified.

GPS coordinates stored as PII in PostgreSQL with pgcrypto — but pgcrypto column-level encryption means the application holds the decryption keys in memory. A backend compromise exposes all patient location history. No mention of key management service (AWS KMS) for column keys.

MQTT/AWS IoT Core device certificates: if a device is stolen, there is no defined certificate revocation workflow. Attacker can impersonate the device, inject false fall events triggering unnecessary emergency dispatch, or suppress real fall events.

OTA firmware update mechanism exists in acceptance criteria but signing/verification design is absent. An unsigned OTA channel on a medical device is a direct patient safety risk — malicious firmware could suppress fall detection entirely.

MobSF scan for mobile app is planned but no runtime application self-protection (RASP) or jailbreak/root detection is specified. On a jailbroken device, screenshot prevention and auto-lock controls are trivially bypassed, exposing patient location and health data.

RapidSOS API credentials stored where? If in Secrets Manager, how are they rotated? If rotation fails and credentials expire, emergency dispatch silently fails — a safety-critical failure mode with no detection mechanism in the plan.

No network segmentation between MQTT ingestion path and caregiver REST API path. A compromised IoT device should not be able to reach the user management or emergency dispatch endpoints. The VPC/WAF design in step 15 does not specify this isolation.
final_review.summary: This is a structurally comprehensive plan that covers the right domains — hardware, firmware, ML, backend, mobile, compliance, and regulatory — but contains multiple fundamental flaws that would block both FDA clearance and safe production deployment. The most critical are: (1) IEC 62366-1 usability engineering is entirely absent, which is a 510(k) submission blocker; (2) the BLE-only connectivity architecture makes the device unsafe when the caregiver's phone is out of range — for a medical safety device this is a design defect, not a limitation; (3) ML validation on lab-collected young-adult simulation data does not support the ≥95% sensitivity claim for the real elderly target population; (4) the 35x25mm PCB with five major ICs and RF modules is likely infeasible without a feasibility analysis; and (5) the 72h GPS battery claim has no supporting power budget. The compliance documentation framework (DHF, ISO 14971, IEC 62304) is well-structured, but is missing the clinical performance data that will be demanded during 510(k) review. The plan reads like a senior engineer's first pass at a regulated medical device — it knows what boxes to check but underestimates the depth required in hardware feasibility, clinical evidence, and usability engineering. Significant rework on the hardware architecture, connectivity design, ML validation methodology, and missing regulatory artifacts is required before this plan could be executed with confidence.

flaws: ML model (step 10) incorrectly depends on firmware (step 9). Model training requires labeled IMU datasets, not working firmware. This serializes 6+ weeks of parallel work unnecessarily and is a fundamental scheduling error.

BLE-only connectivity architecture is a critical safety gap. If the caregiver's phone is out of BLE range (~10m), the device cannot alert anyone. For a medical safety device, this is a design defect — cellular (LTE-M/NB-IoT) fallback is not optional.

PCB form factor constraint (35mm x 25mm) is likely infeasible. u-blox MAX-M10S alone is 9.6mm x 9.6mm. Fitting STM32L476 + nRF52840 + u-blox MAX-M10S + LSM6DSO + BQ25180 + passives on a 35x25mm PCB while meeting RF keepout zones is implausible without a 6-layer board and 0201 passives. No feasibility analysis justifies this constraint.

Dual-chip architecture (STM32L4 + nRF52840 as co-processor) adds unnecessary BOM cost, PCB area, and inter-chip UART latency. The nRF52840 has its own Cortex-M4 that can run FreeRTOS and handle fall detection. This architectural choice is never justified.

72h battery life with active GPS is physically implausible without duty cycling. u-blox MAX-M10S draws ~10-15mA at acquisition, ~1mA tracking. At 5mA average GPS draw alone on a typical 180mAh wristband battery, GPS exhausts battery in 36h before accounting for MCU, BLE, or cellular. No power budget analysis exists anywhere in the plan.

ML model validation uses MobiAct and SisFall datasets — both collected from younger, healthy adults performing simulated falls in controlled lab conditions. Validation on these datasets does not transfer to real elderly users with atypical gait, slower falls, partial falls, or falls from seated positions. This invalidates the ≥95% sensitivity claim for the target population.

IEC 62366-1 usability engineering is completely absent. FDA requires a Usability Engineering File (UEF) for 510(k) submissions. Missing use error risk analysis, formative studies, and summative validation with elderly users is a submission blocker — not a gap that can be patched post-submission.

No post-market surveillance (PMS) plan. FDA 21 CFR Part 803 (MDR) and 21 CFR Part 806 requires PMS from day one of commercialization. A Class II fall detection device with missed-fall risk will face MDR reporting obligations. This is entirely absent.

RapidSOS/NG911 integration assumes a contractual API relationship that takes months to establish, has geographic coverage gaps in rural US, and requires specific data formatting per NENA i3 standards. Treating it as a simple API call in step 12 acceptance criteria is unrealistic.

Software Safety Class assignment says 'Class B minimum' without justification. For a device where software failure (missed fall) could lead to serious patient harm (death from undetected fall), IEC 62304 Class C is the defensible classification. Class B underclassification risks FDA refusal-to-accept.

MQTT message buffering during connectivity loss is tested in step 19 but never designed. Step 9 firmware spec says nothing about flash-backed event queue, queue depth, TTL, or replay ordering. The system test will fail because the feature was never specified.

No manufacturing process validation. A Class II medical device requires IQ/OQ/PQ for manufacturing processes (pick-and-place, reflow, IP67 seal testing, functional test fixture). This is entirely absent from the plan — the DHF cannot be complete without it.

JWT authentication for IoT devices (step 4, step 12) conflicts with mutual TLS mentioned in the same plan. AWS IoT Core uses X.509 certificates, not JWTs, for device authentication. The authentication architecture is internally inconsistent.

No device provisioning security model at manufacturing time. How are X.509 certificates injected at the factory? No HSM, no certificate authority, no secure element (ATECC608 or equivalent). Firmware signing is listed as an acceptance criterion but the key management infrastructure is never designed.
suggestions: Move step 10 (ML model) to run in parallel with steps 6-9, with only the deployment-to-firmware step blocked on step 9. This recovers 3-4 weeks of critical path time.

Add LTE-M or NB-IoT as the primary alert channel, with BLE as the configuration/pairing interface only. Nordic nRF9161 or Sequans Monarch handles LTE-M and integrates more cleanly than the dual-chip STM32+nRF52840 design.

Commission a 6-layer PCB stack-up analysis before committing to the 35x25mm constraint. If the constraint is from product design requirements, add a feasibility step before PCB layout. If it is aspirational, state it as a target and validate in step 7 (mechanical).

Add an explicit power budget analysis step before PCB design (step 6). Define GPS duty cycle (e.g., 1 fix per 30s when stationary, continuous when motion detected), CPU active/sleep ratios, and BLE advertising intervals. This gates the 72h claim.

Add step 0.5: IEC 62366-1 Usability Engineering Plan, with formative usability studies at step 5 (UX Design) using actual elderly participants, and summative validation scheduled before step 20 (DHF completion).

Add a clinical performance validation step between step 17 (embedded test) and step 20 (DHF). The 510(k) substantial equivalence argument for a fall detection device will be strengthened significantly by even a 30-subject pilot study with elderly users in a care home setting.

Add step 8.5: device identity and certificate provisioning design. Specify secure element (ATECC608B or equivalent), certificate authority architecture, factory provisioning fixture, and certificate revocation mechanism.

Replace IEC 62443 as the primary cybersecurity standard with FDA's 2023 Cybersecurity Guidance (Refuse to Accept policy) and NIST IR 8259. IEC 62443 is industrial control systems — reference it as supplementary only.

Define HIPAA Business Associate Agreements (BAAs) with AWS, Twilio, SendGrid, and RapidSOS explicitly. AWS BAA is available but must be executed. Twilio and SendGrid have their own BAA processes. Missing BAA voids HIPAA safe harbor.

Specify a post-market surveillance plan as a deliverable in step 21 (regulatory). Include MDR decision tree, complaint handling procedure (21 CFR Part 820.198), and field corrective action trigger criteria.
missing: IEC 62366-1 Usability Engineering File — formative studies, use error analysis, summative validation with elderly target users. FDA will not accept a 510(k) without this.
Post-market surveillance plan (21 CFR Part 803, 21 CFR Part 806, EU MDR Article 83 if international scope intended).
Cellular (LTE-M/NB-IoT) connectivity design. BLE range limitation makes the device unsafe as a sole-communication-path medical device.
Power budget analysis and GPS duty cycling strategy supporting the 72h battery life claim.
Manufacturing process validation (IQ/OQ/PQ) for PCB assembly, IP67 seal testing, and final functional test.
Secure element and certificate provisioning architecture for device identity at manufacturing time.
Clinical performance data with elderly subjects — lab datasets alone are insufficient for the 510(k) substantial equivalence performance testing section.
HIPAA BAA execution plan for all third-party data processors (AWS, Twilio, SendGrid, RapidSOS).
Data retention and right-to-deletion policy (HIPAA minimum 6-year retention, GDPR if EU market).
PCB feasibility analysis before committing to 35x25mm constraint.
Alert deduplication logic — what prevents duplicate NG911 dispatch if caregiver and auto-dispatch both trigger within seconds?
Firmware OTA update design (mentioned in step 9 acceptance criteria but never designed as a task — rollback, signature verification, atomic swap, and fail-safe boot are all unspecified).
International regulatory pathway — CE marking under EU MDR 2017/745 is not mentioned despite the device's clear EU market applicability.
security_risks: BLE bonding without a secure element means pairing keys are stored in MCU flash. A physical device compromise yields all pairing credentials and enables location tracking of the patient. No mention of anti-tamper or key erasure on tamper detection.
JWT tokens for caregiver mobile app without refresh token rotation policy. A stolen JWT provides persistent patient location access. Token lifetime and rotation strategy are unspecified.

GPS coordinates stored as PII in PostgreSQL with pgcrypto — but pgcrypto column-level encryption means the application holds the decryption keys in memory. A backend compromise exposes all patient location history. No mention of key management service (AWS KMS) for column keys.

MQTT/AWS IoT Core device certificates: if a device is stolen, there is no defined certificate revocation workflow. Attacker can impersonate the device, inject false fall events triggering unnecessary emergency dispatch, or suppress real fall events.

OTA firmware update mechanism exists in acceptance criteria but signing/verification design is absent. An unsigned OTA channel on a medical device is a direct patient safety risk — malicious firmware could suppress fall detection entirely.

MobSF scan for mobile app is planned but no runtime application self-protection (RASP) or jailbreak/root detection is specified. On a jailbroken device, screenshot prevention and auto-lock controls are trivially bypassed, exposing patient location and health data.

RapidSOS API credentials stored where? If in Secrets Manager, how are they rotated? If rotation fails and credentials expire, emergency dispatch silently fails — a safety-critical failure mode with no detection mechanism in the plan.

No network segmentation between MQTT ingestion path and caregiver REST API path. A compromised IoT device should not be able to reach the user management or emergency dispatch endpoints. The VPC/WAF design in step 15 does not specify this isolation.
history.summary: This is a structurally comprehensive plan that covers the right domains — hardware, firmware, ML, backend, mobile, compliance, and regulatory — but contains multiple fundamental flaws that would block both FDA clearance and safe production deployment. The most critical are: (1) IEC 62366-1 usability engineering is entirely absent, which is a 510(k) submission blocker; (2) the BLE-only connectivity architecture makes the device unsafe when the caregiver's phone is out of range — for a medical safety device this is a design defect, not a limitation; (3) ML validation on lab-collected young-adult simulation data does not support the ≥95% sensitivity claim for the real elderly target population; (4) the 35x25mm PCB with five major ICs and RF modules is likely infeasible without a feasibility analysis; and (5) the 72h GPS battery claim has no supporting power budget. The compliance documentation framework (DHF, ISO 14971, IEC 62304) is well-structured, but is missing the clinical performance data that will be demanded during 510(k) review. The plan reads like a senior engineer's first pass at a regulated medical device — it knows what boxes to check but underestimates the depth required in hardware feasibility, clinical evidence, and usability engineering. Significant rework on the hardware architecture, connectivity design, ML validation methodology, and missing regulatory artifacts is required before this plan could be executed with confidence.
